items.prompt: Solve `x + 7 = 19` and show how each step preserves equality.
items.expected_evidence: identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases with mathematically valid structure.

items.prompt: Solve `4x = 28`, then check the solution by substitution.
items.expected_evidence: complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic with mathematically valid structure.

items.prompt: Match the equation `n - 5 = 12` to a balance or missing-value situation and solve it.
items.expected_evidence: justify why equal additive differences do not by themselves establish proportionality
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner demonstrates justify why equal additive differences do not by themselves establish proportionality with mathematically valid structure.

items.prompt: Explain why subtracting the same value from both sides of an equation preserves the solution set.
items.expected_evidence: identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner explains the structure coherently and ties the explanation back to identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases.

items.prompt: Explain what the variable represents and why inverse operations help isolate it.
items.expected_evidence: complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner explains the structure coherently and ties the explanation back to complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic.

items.prompt: A student solves `x + 5 = 13` by writing `x = 13 + 5`. Diagnose the first structural mistake. Focus on additive pattern misclassification.
items.expected_evidence: identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner identifies the dominant breakdown, names the structural issue accurately, and redirects the work toward identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases.

items.prompt: A student solves `3x = 18` by dividing only the `x`. Explain what went wrong. Focus on scale invariance breakdown.
items.expected_evidence: complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner identifies the dominant breakdown, names the structural issue accurately, and redirects the work toward complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic.

items.prompt: A gym charges a $12 signup fee plus $5 per class. Write and solve an equation for the number of classes if the total was $32.
items.expected_evidence: identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner preserves identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases in a changed surface form or representation.

items.prompt: A balance model has three equal bags and 6 loose counters on one side and 24 counters on the other. Write and solve the equation.
items.expected_evidence: complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner preserves complete missing values in proportional tables using multiplicative logic in a changed surface form or representation.

items.prompt: Two weeks later, solve `m/3 = 8` and explain how you know the result is reasonable.
items.expected_evidence: identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner still demonstrates identify whether paired quantities scale proportionally in bounded cases on a delayed recheck without a blocking recurrence.

items.prompt: Before solving proportional relationships, complete a prerequisite check on unit rate reasoning and explain how it supports the current equation work.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether unit rate reasoning is secure enough to support proportional relationships
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in unit rate reasoning from a current-node issue in proportional relationships.

items.prompt: Use a quick multiplicative comparison and scaling check to explain whether the learner is blocked by prerequisite structure or by proportional relationships itself.
items.expected_evidence: shows whether multiplicative comparison and scaling is secure enough to support proportional relationships
items.scoring_guidance: Meets when the learner response makes it possible to distinguish an upstream gap in multiplicative comparison and scaling from a current-node issue in proportional relationships.
